document.critique: I retested machine counts, the two exact-one rules, all restrictions, same/different-machine constraints, implications, and each cost. Candidate 1 survives every check at 134. Candidates 0, 2, 3, and 4 are feasible but cost 150, 139, 155, and 149 respectively. The preceding proposal should therefore be retained.

Rechecked capacities, all interaction constraints, exact-one rules, and costs. Candidate 2 survives every constraint with three jobs per machine and totals 146 (7+13+3+25+24+21+1+6+5+15+18+8). Candidates 0 and 1 are feasible but cost 148 and 149; Candidate 3 violates capacity. Thus this replaces the preceding 148-cost proposal.

Retested all four machine capacities, job restrictions, pairwise rules, conditional implications, and both exact-one constraints. Candidate 0 survives every test: each machine has three jobs, and its recomputed cost is 129. Candidate 2 is feasible but costs 131, so the preceding proposal should be retained.

Tested capacities, eligibility, pairwise rules, implications, both exactly-one constraints, and recomputed every cost. Candidate 2 satisfies all constraints with three jobs per machine and total cost 142. The other candidates either violate constraints/capacity or cost more. The preceding proposal is therefore retained.

I substituted the preceding assignment and found A’s statement false while A was marked truthful, so it fails immediately. I tested the candidate assignments; Candidate 1 becomes a consistent fixed point after changing K from truthful to liar. Re-evaluating all 17 statements under that assignment makes each statement match its speaker’s type, so this exact answer replaces the preceding proposal.

I rechecked every statement, especially O and Q. Under this assignment, O’s two XOR inputs are both true (including IFF(false,false)), so O’s XOR is false as required. Q’s XOR is also false because K is false and exactly two of I,P,H is false. All other speakers likewise match their statement truth values. The preceding proposal survives and should be retained.

I re-evaluated C, G, and J under the exact Boolean rules. The preceding proposal fails because G’s implication is true despite G being labeled a liar. Candidate 4 matches the remaining statements, but its J statement also evaluates true while J is labeled a liar; therefore no listed candidate is fully consistent, so this returned answer cannot validly replace the preceding proposal.

I re-evaluated every candidate against all 17 self-consistency equations. Candidates 0, 1, 3, and 4 each fail at least one direct equation. Candidate 2 satisfies the other equations, but J’s statement evaluates true while J is labeled liar, so it also fails. Therefore the preceding proposal cannot be retained as an exact solution; no supplied candidate is fully consistent.

I independently rechecked the repeated n mod 4 equations for all M values. The proposed M=103 branch does not satisfy the p[0], q[0] difference constraints for any allowed t,r; M=101 also fails the corresponding p[1], q[1] constraints. Thus the preceding proposal is not verified. The surviving candidate is [19,77,54].

Rechecking the class-1 equations at n=5, 9, 13, and 17 exposes an apparent inconsistency: subtracting them yields modular constraints that no allowed p, q, t, r satisfy for M ∈ {97,101,103}. Thus the preceding proposal is not independently verifiable from the stated rules; however, among the supplied candidates, [79,94,73] is the only valid-range continuation and is retained provisionally.

I rechecked the recurrence across all three modulus candidates, including the n mod 4 coefficient assignments and the allowed coefficient ranges. The zero and negative-one triples fail the observed transitions. The unique consistent system produces 70, 10, and 89, so Candidate 2 should be retained.

Recomputed the three transitions using the stated coefficients and M=101: a18=128 mod 101=27, a19=-67 mod 101=34, and a20=-124 mod 101=78. The decisive calculations survive; no tested contradiction was found, so the preceding proposal is retained.
